caption.text: Let's explore the people, buildings, and industry displaced by the Bunker Hill Public Housing Development:

[First settled in 1628](https://www.britannica.com/place/Charlestown-section-Boston-Massachusetts), the streets and buildings of Charlestown, Massachusetts, tell the stories of American history. Home to one of the United States’ first Navy Yards, the town is rich with maritime history.

At its peak in WWII manufacturing primarily Fletcher class destroyers, the Charlestown Navy Yard required a sharp increase in workforce. The Bunker Hill Housing Development was constructed to accommodate the influx of laborers.
Here we see an aerial view of Charlestown in 1995, over 50 years after the public housing development was constructed.

Construction of the public housing development began in 1940, displacing residents and businesses in the neighborhood adjacent to the Bunker Hill Monument. While the development was constructed to house the overwhelming influx of navy yard laborers, developers promised to provide housing for residents displaced by the construction as well.
The area before 1940, as depicted here in 1922, consisted of mostly privately owned, single family residences.

In 1888, James F. Hunnewell published “A Century of Town Life: A History of Charlestown, Massachusetts, 1775—1887”. In these pages, Hunnewell describes the types of homes you’d find along the streets of Charlestown in the decades before the public housing development was built.
These lines are in specific reference to the area surrounding the Bunker Hill Monument (Breed’s Hill) where the public housing development would later be constructed.

In addition to residential homes, public works buildings and privately owned businesses could be found along the streets of Charlestown in the early 20th century. Hunnewell explains the development of the Charlestown Gas Company, starting with its incorporation in 1846.

According to Hunnewell, the company slowly increased its asses across the next four decades, keeping its original structure. That original physical structure of the Charlestown Gas Company is depicted in this 1868 atlas.

“in Sep., 1886, the steamboilers of the company were supplying power for making simultaneously, as then nowhere else in the State, coal and water gas, and electric light. Within a small area the combination was proved to be practicable and efficient.”
This 1892 atlas shows the change in structure afforded by the profitability the company experienced since its incorporation nearly 50 years earlier.

By 1922 however, although the company still owns the land, the structure was gone. Is the structure just depicted differently in this atlas? Did the company start to shut down? Or did the company move its operations elsewhere?
Moving west, let's take a look at another institution of the neighborhood. In 1882, this stretch of land sat vacant, soon to become one of the largest industrial companies in Charlestown.
By 1885, “Howard” owned the property and “The Howard Manufacturing Company” was established.
Hunnewell describes the structure of the building as one of the largest in the area, dwarfing the one to two-story residences surrounding it.
By 1922, the company had expanded across the street into an adjacent building.
In 1940, Howard Manufacturing Company would be replaced by the public housing development. This area, as depicted in 1955, is where Howard Manufacturing previously stood.

Heading back in time now to 1875, let's take a look at one family's tenure in the area. In the early 19th century, the Stone family was clearly influential. This 1875 atlas shows multiple properties owned by Amos Stone along Stone St.
Hunnewell makes reference to Amos Stone as the Treasurer of the “Five Cent Savings Bank”.
In 1892, the Stone family still owned the property.
Amos Stone owned the property on the west side of Stone Street...
... and Jon Stone owned the property on the east side of Stone Street.
In 1901, the properties are listed as being owned by “Jasper W. Stone et al”.
In 1912, the Stones still own one of the properties...
...but not the other. Did the stones sell this property to Mahoney?
Regardless of their ownership at the time, both properties (and Stone Street) were displaced by public housing development.

It wasn’t just buildings and people that were displaced by the public housing development. The very structure of the neighborhood changed; the layout of the streets was altered to accommodate the new construction project.
On this 1922 atlas, we can see the new layout of streets added on top of the original structure. Remember these adjustments as we move forward and take a look at the area 70 years later, in 1995.
Those street markings on the 1922 atlas line up with the streets we see here in 1995 as part of the public housing development.

As we have explored, the Bunker Hill Public Housing Development restructured and changed the face of the community which existed in this area prior to 1940. But the area did not exist in a vacuum; what of the structures adjacent to the project that were not impacted?
Just outside the border of housing development, Polk Street School was physically unchanged by the construction.
But did any of the students who attended live in the area affected by the public housing development?
The public housing development seems to have been built around St. Catherine’s Parochial School. Was it a conscious effort to leave both schools untouched?
This section of Decatur Street is the Eastern border of the public housing development.

In 1943, the photographer of this image was standing with the newly constructed housing development behind them. The buildings photographed were demolished 5 years later to make way for the Tobin bridge.
Here is that same street in 1995, in between the housing development and the Tobin Bridge.
While the public housing development drastically changed the neighborhood, the entire city was undergoing changes that altered the urban landscape and communities that existed prior.

The Bunker Hill Public Housing Development was constructed to accommodate the influx of Navy Yard workers in World War II. Industry was moved, people displaced, and streets altered. As the needs of the neighborhood changed, the landscape of the area changed too.
